summary: Gruber argues that Apple's iPhone strategy since 2017 has been a deliberate two-tier system that most observers fail to fully appreciate. The iPhone X marked not just the shift to Face ID, but the beginning of separate Pro and non-Pro product lines with fundamentally different lifecycle strategies. Pro models use cutting-edge components and are produced for just one year, while non-Pro models use refined, year-old technology and remain in production for 2-3 additional years at reduced prices. The iPhone 14's dramatically improved repairability, despite using the older A15 chip, exemplifies this: it was designed for longevity in the lineup. Gruber contends the non-Pro iPhones are the unsung workhorses of Apple's ecosystem, essential even if they lack the Pro models' excitement.
key_points: The iPhone X in 2017 marked the start of Apple's two-tier iPhone strategy, not just the switch to Face ID
Pro iPhones are produced for only one year then discontinued, while non-Pro models stay in the lineup for 2-3 additional years at declining prices
The iPhone 14's internal redesign for repairability, despite keeping the A15 chip, reveals Apple deliberately engineering non-Pro models for multi-year production
The iPhone 14 Plus's reportedly weak first-year sales don't indicate failure because non-Pro models are designed to be workhorses in years two and three at lower prices
Apple's strategy splits innovation (Pro) from accessibility and longevity (non-Pro), and both are essential to the ecosystem
money_quotes: The iPhones that are the most expensive, most cutting-edge, and I presume the hardest to manufacture are only produced for one single year.
The chip remained the same but the internal design was altogether different, and better. The iPhone 14 is a design that was meant to remain in production for years to come.
The iPhones Pro are far more exciting, but the non-pro iPhones are essential to the lineup and thus to the entire iPhone ecosystem.
In sports terms, the Pro models are the offense, and the non-pro models are the defense. The offense gets the glory, but it's defense, they say, that wins championships.
To me, the workhorse years for non-pro iPhones are years two and three in the lineup, when they come down in price.
I find that fascinating, but it's seldom remarked upon.
key_insight: Apple's real iPhone strategy isn't about the exciting Pro models but about engineering non-Pro models for multi-year production longevity, creating an ecosystem where cutting-edge innovation and long-term accessibility serve complementary roles.
